era_context: The Roman Empire under Tiberius in his final years. Judea under Roman procuratorial rule following the deposition of Herod Archelaus in 6 CE. The early Christian movement in its nascent phase, centered in Jerusalem and beginning to spread to diaspora communities. Han Dynasty China under Guangwu, consolidating power after the collapse of the Western Han and the brief Xin Dynasty.

events.title: Pontius Pilate Removed as Procurator of Judea
events.description: Pontius Pilate, the Roman procurator of Judea, was removed from office in 36 CE following complaints from the Jewish population and the Samaritan population about his harsh rule. According to Josephus, Pilate had ordered a massacre of Samaritans who had gathered on Mount Gerizim, and this incident prompted his removal. Pilate was recalled to Rome and replaced by Marcellus. This event marked the end of Pilate's controversial tenure as procurator (26–36 CE).
events.certainty_note: Josephus provides a detailed account of Pilate's removal. The date of 36 CE is well-established in the historical record.

events.title: Herod Agrippa I Imprisoned in Rome
events.description: Herod Agrippa I was imprisoned in Rome in 36 CE (or possibly 38 CE, according to some sources) on charges of treason by Tiberius. Agrippa had allegedly made remarks critical of Tiberius and was arrested. He remained imprisoned until Caligula's accession in 37 CE, when he was released and granted the tetrarchy of Philip. The exact dating of his imprisonment is uncertain.
events.certainty_note: The dating of Agrippa's imprisonment is uncertain; sources vary between 36 and 38 CE. His release and elevation are more securely dated to 37 CE.

events.title: Philip the Tetrarch Dies
events.description: Philip, the tetrarch of the northeastern region of Judea (the Tetrarchy of Philip), died in 33 or 34 CE (some sources place his death in 36 CE). His territory was initially placed under direct Roman administration but was later granted to Herod Agrippa I by Caligula in 37 CE. Philip's death marked the end of the Herodian dynasty's direct rule in this region.
events.certainty_note: The dating of Philip's death is uncertain; sources vary between 33 and 36 CE. Josephus is the primary source, but his dating is not always precise.

events.title: Han Dynasty Consolidation Under Guangwu
events.description: Emperor Guangwu of Han continued his consolidation of power over the fragmented territories of China. By 36 CE, the Eastern Han Dynasty had achieved relative stability and territorial control over most of the former Han territories. The period was marked by the restoration of Confucian scholarship, the strengthening of the civil service system, and the reorganization of provincial administration.

sources.name: Hanshu and Later Han historiographic records

events.certainty_note: The general chronology of Guangwu's reign is well-established in Chinese historical records.

events.title: Early Christian Missionary Activity Expands
events.description: By 36 CE, the early Christian movement had begun to expand beyond Jerusalem into diaspora communities in Judea and the surrounding regions. The conversion of the Ethiopian eunuch, as recorded in Acts 8, is traditionally dated to this period, symbolizing the movement's expansion beyond Jewish communities. The apostle Philip is credited with this conversion in Acts.
events.certainty_note: The dating of early Christian missionary activity is uncertain and often traditional. Acts was written decades after the events it describes. The year 36 CE is assigned by convention within the broader period of early Christian expansion (30s–50s CE).

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Philip's death is disputed; sources vary between 33 and 36 CE. The dating of Herod Agrippa I's imprisonment is uncertain; some sources place it in 38 CE rather than 36 CE. The historicity of the Ethiopian eunuch conversion narrative in Acts is debated by scholars, with some viewing it as a theological construct rather than a historical event. Pilate's removal is well-attested, but the exact circumstances and the extent of his responsibility for the Samaritan massacre are debated.
historiographic_note: The year 36 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire and Judea through later historical sources, particularly Josephus. Chinese sources provide reliable documentation of Han Dynasty affairs. Early Christian history relies heavily on the Acts of the Apostles, written 30–40 years after the events, introducing significant dating uncertainty. The perspective of sources on Pilate is often biased, as he was unpopular with the Jewish population and later Christian sources portrayed him negatively.

graph_edges.note: Pilate's removal occurred in the same year as Caligula's accession
Agrippa's imprisonment led to his release and appointment under Caligula
Philip's death led to Agrippa's appointment to his territory
